All of the above

Futures are traded on exchanges with clearing corporations. This reduces counterparty risk and settlement issues compared to forward contracts.

flashcards.front: Loss from derivatives trading on recognized exchanges can be carried forward for 12 years. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Such losses can be carried forward for 8 assessment years, not 12 years.

flashcards.front: A short call option position can be closed by taking a long put option with same strike price and expiry. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

A short call can be closed only by taking a long call option with the same strike price and expiry, not a put option.

flashcards.front: Which complaint can the exchange consider for redressal?
flashcards.back:
Excess brokerage charged

Exchanges handle complaints related to trades executed on their platform. Excess brokerage charged by a trading member is covered.

flashcards.front: A person buys a call option at Rs. 20 with strike price Rs. 350. On expiry, stock price is Rs. 310. What will he do?
flashcards.back:
Exercise the option

Since market price is lower than strike price, exercising the call gives no benefit. His loss is limited to the premium paid.

flashcards.front: Trading members must maintain higher capital adequacy than clearing members. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Clearing members settle trades and take higher responsibility. Therefore, they must maintain higher capital adequacy.

flashcards.front: A trader sells a call option at Rs. 12 premium with strike price Rs. 200. What is his maximum possible loss?
flashcards.back:
Unlimited

If the stock price rises sharply, the seller must deliver at strike price. Since price can rise indefinitely, loss can be unlimited.

flashcards.front: Securities Transaction Tax (STT) is charged on:
flashcards.back:
Both purchase of equity shares and sale of derivatives

STT is charged on both buying and selling of equity shares. For derivatives, it is charged only on sell side transactions.

flashcards.front: Who decides the lot size in futures contracts?
flashcards.back:
Stock exchange

The stock exchange fixes the contract size (lot size) to standardize trading.

flashcards.front: In options trading, the seller pays the premium at the time of entering the contract. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

The buyer pays the premium to the seller. The seller receives the premium for taking the obligation.

flashcards.front: In which contract is gain or loss settled daily through mark-to-market?
flashcards.back:
Futures contracts

Futures contracts are marked to market daily. Profit or loss is settled every trading day.

flashcards.front: When the near month contract expires in a calendar spread, the far month position becomes:
flashcards.back:
Naked position

Once one leg expires, the remaining contract becomes an open (naked) position.

flashcards.front: The main objective of derivatives is to:
flashcards.back:
Manage risk

Derivatives help transfer and manage risk from those who want to avoid it to those willing to take it.

flashcards.front: If interest rates increase, call option premium increases. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Higher interest rates increase the value of call options and decrease the value of put options.

flashcards.front: An option buyer has:
flashcards.back:
Right only

The option buyer has the right but not the obligation to exercise the contract.

flashcards.front: Trading members in derivatives segment are not required to register with SEBI. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Trading members must register with SEBI to operate in both cash and derivatives segments.

flashcards.front: Futures trading allows settlement without physical delivery because of:
flashcards.back:
Cash settlement

Cash settlement allows contracts to be settled by paying price differences instead of delivering the asset.

flashcards.front: On NSE, when does the April index futures series begin trading?
flashcards.back:
Last Friday of January

NSE maintains three active series at a time. When the January contract expires on the last Thursday, the April series starts trading on the next day (last Friday of January).

flashcards.front: Operational risk mainly refers to losses caused by:
flashcards.back:
Inadequate contingency planning

Operational risk arises from internal failures such as poor planning, system errors, or fraud. It relates to internal processes rather than external natural events.

flashcards.front: Open interest means the total number of outstanding contracts in the market. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Open interest shows the total number of unsettled contracts at a particular time. It indicates market participation and liquidity.

flashcards.front: Clearing corporations can use client margin money to settle a clearing member’s own dues. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Client funds are separate and cannot be used for a clearing member’s personal or proprietary obligations.

flashcards.front: A clearing member deposits Rs. 75 lakhs as liquid assets. Minimum required net worth is Rs. 50 lakhs. What margin is available? (in lakhs)
flashcards.back:
25

Margin available = 75 − 50 = 25 lakhs. The excess over minimum requirement is used as trading margin.

flashcards.front: An efficient cash market is necessary for an efficient futures market. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Futures prices are based on cash market prices. If the cash market is inefficient, futures pricing will also be affected.

flashcards.front: If futures price increases, the short position holder’s mark-to-market account is credited. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

When price rises, short position holders incur loss. Therefore, their margin account is debited, not credited.

flashcards.front: Capital adequacy requirement for derivative brokers is same as cash market brokers. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Derivatives involve higher risk due to leverage. Hence, capital adequacy requirements are higher than in the cash market.

flashcards.front: Rho measures change in option premium due to change in:
flashcards.back:
Risk-free interest rate

Rho measures sensitivity of option price to changes in the risk-free interest rate.

flashcards.front: The Ask price represents:
flashcards.back:
Price seller is willing to sell at

Ask price is the selling price quoted by the seller. It is the price at which the trader is ready to sell.

flashcards.front: Futures and options on individual stocks in India are allowed on:
flashcards.back:
Selected stocks meeting exchange criteria

Only stocks that meet SEBI and exchange eligibility criteria are allowed in the derivatives segment.

flashcards.front: Higher volatility leads to higher initial margin requirement. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Higher volatility increases risk. Exchanges demand higher margins to protect against price fluctuations.

flashcards.front: Net worth requirement for a clearing member is higher than that for a non-clearing member. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Clearing members settle trades for others. Hence, they must maintain higher financial strength.

flashcards.front: Buying a put option on shares already owned is called:
flashcards.back:
Protective put

A protective put limits downside risk of owned shares by providing insurance against price fall.

flashcards.front: All active exchange members must contribute to the Trade Guarantee Fund. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Trade Guarantee Fund ensures settlement security. All active members contribute to maintain market confidence.

flashcards.front: Increase in interest rates will:
flashcards.back:
Decrease put premium

Higher interest rates increase call values but reduce put option values.

flashcards.front: In a forward contract, the party agreeing to sell in future takes:
flashcards.back:
Short position

The seller in a forward contract takes a short position, agreeing to deliver the asset at a future date.

flashcards.front: Exposure of a trading member with clients holding 12 long and 10 short futures contracts will be:
flashcards.back:
Grossed to 22 contracts

Trading member exposure is calculated by grossing all client positions, not netting them.

flashcards.front: If market price is below strike price, a call option will:
flashcards.back:
Expire worthless

A call option is worthless if market price is below strike price since buying from market is cheaper.

flashcards.front: Option premium is affected by difference between spot price and strike price. State Yes or No.
flashcards.back:
Yes

The intrinsic value depends on difference between spot and strike price. Hence, premium changes with price movement.

flashcards.front: Higher initial margin collection improves the financial strength of the clearing corporation. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Higher margins reduce default risk. This improves the solvency and financial safety of the clearing corporation.

flashcards.front: Futures contracts are generally identified by their delivery month. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Futures contracts are named after their expiry month (e.g., March contract, April contract). This month is called the contract month.

flashcards.front: Mr. A sold a put option with strike Rs. 400 at premium Rs. 32. Lot size is 500. Stock closes at Rs. 350 on expiry. What is his net result?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 9,000 loss

Loss per share = 400 − 350 = 50. Net loss per share = 50 − 32 = 18. Total loss = 18 × 500 = Rs. 9,000.

flashcards.front: If tick size is 0.2 index point and multiplier is Rs. 50, what is value of one tick?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 100

Tick value = 0.2 × 50 = Rs. 10. Each tick movement results in Rs. 10 profit or loss.

flashcards.front: Securities deposited by clearing members as liquid assets are:
flashcards.back:
Marked to market periodically

Such securities are marked to market regularly to reflect current value and ensure adequate collateral coverage.

flashcards.front: Contract month refers to:
flashcards.back:
Expiry month of futures contract

Contract month is the month in which the futures contract expires.

flashcards.front: Initial margin is calculated based on:
flashcards.back:
Value at Risk (VaR) method

Initial margin is calculated using Value at Risk (VaR), usually at 99% confidence level for one-day horizon.

flashcards.front: Daily trading price limits define the maximum percentage price movement allowed in a day. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Price limits restrict excessive daily price movement to control volatility and protect investors.

flashcards.front: Index-based derivatives are more suitable than individual stock derivatives for portfolio hedging. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Index derivatives provide broader market coverage and better liquidity, making them cost-effective for hedging diversified portfolios.

flashcards.front: A Trading-cum-Clearing Member is responsible for his trades and also trades of members under him. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

A Trading-cum-Clearing Member clears his own trades as well as those of trading members associated with him.

flashcards.front: Default in derivatives segment is not treated as default in cash segment. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Default in one segment is treated as default across all segments of the exchange.

flashcards.front: High margins increase the cost of derivatives trading. State Yes or No.
flashcards.back:
Yes

Higher margin requirement blocks more funds, making trading more expensive.

flashcards.front: Risk specific to a company or industry that can be reduced by diversification is:
flashcards.back:
Systematic Risk

Unsystematic risk is company-specific risk and can be reduced by diversifying the portfolio.

flashcards.front: Clearing of trades on stock exchange is done by:
flashcards.back:
Clearing members

Clearing members handle settlement and clearing of trades. Trading members cannot clear trades independently.

flashcards.front: A put option is In-The-Money when:
flashcards.back:
Strike price is higher than market price

A put option is profitable when strike price is higher than current market price.

flashcards.front: Delta measures change in option premium due to change in:
flashcards.back:
Spot price

Delta shows how much option premium changes for a small change in underlying asset price.

flashcards.front: A put option is Out-of-The-Money when:
flashcards.back:
Strike price is lower than market price

A put is OTM when market price is higher than strike price, so exercising gives no benefit.

flashcards.front: Diversification can eliminate systematic risk. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Systematic risk affects the entire market and cannot be removed by diversification.

flashcards.front: A trader sells futures at Rs. 354 and buys back at Rs. 341. Lot size 900. What is profit?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 11,700

Profit per unit = 354 − 341 = 13. Total profit = 13 × 900 = Rs. 11,700.

flashcards.front: A trader makes profit in a short September futures position when:
flashcards.back:
He squares off by buying September futures at lower price

In a short position, profit occurs when price falls. The trader must buy back the same expiry (September) futures at a lower price to earn profit.

flashcards.front: Which of the following is NOT an application of stock market indices?
flashcards.back:
Private equity funds

Private equity funds are not linked to stock market indices and are not traded on stock exchanges.

flashcards.front: Option contracts are not symmetrical in terms of rights and obligations. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Option buyers have rights without obligation, while sellers have obligation with unlimited risk. Hence, obligations are not equal.

flashcards.front: Intrinsic value and time value of a call option can never be negative. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
True

Intrinsic value is either positive or zero. Time value also cannot be negative, so total premium cannot be negative.

flashcards.front: Daily mark-to-market settlement applies to:
flashcards.back:
Futures contracts

Futures contracts are settled daily through mark-to-market adjustment of margins.

flashcards.front: Market price of share is Rs. 120 and strike price of call is Rs. 110. Option premium is Rs. 24. What is intrinsic value?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 10

Intrinsic value = Spot − Strike = 120 − 110 = Rs. 10. Remaining premium is time value.

flashcards.front: The main objective of position limits is to:
flashcards.back:
Prevent market manipulation by large participants

Position limits prevent any single trader from taking very large positions and influencing market prices unfairly.

flashcards.front: A seller of a put option gains when the underlying price:
flashcards.back:
Both B and C

A put seller earns premium and benefits if price rises or remains stable. Loss occurs only when price falls significantly.

flashcards.front: A portfolio of 50 stocks is twice as risky as a portfolio of 100 stocks. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Diversification reduces risk up to a point. Increasing stocks from 50 to 100 reduces very little additional risk.

flashcards.front: Buying a call with lower strike and selling a call with higher strike (same expiry) is called:
flashcards.back:
Bull spread

A bull call spread is created by buying a lower strike call and selling a higher strike call, expecting moderate price rise.

flashcards.front: Futures trading is riskier than equity trading mainly due to:
flashcards.back:
High leverage

Futures allow large exposure with small margin (leverage). This increases both profit potential and risk.

flashcards.front: Institutional investors pay higher margins than individual investors in derivatives trading. State True or False.
flashcards.back:
False

Margin requirements are based on risk and position size, not on whether the investor is institutional or individual.

flashcards.front: Brokers are required to make their clients understand the risks involved in trading derivatives and get a copy of which document signed by their clients at the time of client on-boarding?
flashcards.back:
Risk Disclosure Document

Signing the Risk Disclosure Document serves as acknowledgment by the client that they have been informed about the risks associated with derivatives trading.

flashcards.front: The main risks involved in trading in the equity derivatives market include Market risk, Liquidity risk, and:
flashcards.back:
Counterparty risk

While Market and Liquidity risks are prominent, Counterparty risk (though mitigated by clearing corporations in exchange-traded derivatives) is also a significant consideration in finance.

flashcards.front: Which risk is generally not applicable to clients trading in exchange-traded equity derivatives because settlement is guaranteed by the exchange/clearing corporation?
flashcards.back:
Counterparty risk

For exchange-traded derivatives, the clearing corporation acts as the central counterparty, eliminating the risk of default by the original counterparty to the trade.

flashcards.front: Why are the risks of trading equity derivatives heightened compared to trading the underlying asset?
flashcards.back:
Leveraged nature of contracts

Derivatives require only a fraction of the contract's value as margin or premium, meaning a small price move can lead to a large percentage gain or loss on the initial investment.

flashcards.front: An investor who is less risk averse would likely prefer to have greater exposure to:
flashcards.back:
Equity and other risky investments

Less risk-averse investors are more comfortable with potential losses and are therefore more willing to invest in assets like stocks that have higher potential returns but also higher risk.

flashcards.front: Which of the following is listed as a parameter on which one's risk tolerance can depend?
flashcards.back:
Combined family income

Factors like income, age, number of dependents, and financial situation influence an individual's capacity and willingness to take investment risks.

flashcards.front: What type of order gets activated when a trigger price is reached or crossed, and then enters the market as a market or limit order?
flashcards.back:
Stop-loss order

A Stop-loss order is designed to limit potential losses by automatically placing a trade once a specific price level is breached.

flashcards.front: In an order driven market like Indian F&O platforms, orders are matched automatically on which basis?
flashcards.back:
Price time priority

The trading system first matches orders based on the best price (highest bid, lowest ask), and then within the same price, it prioritizes orders based on when they were entered.

flashcards.front: For order matching, the best buy order is the one with the:
flashcards.back:
Highest price

The system wants to match sellers with the highest possible prices offered by buyers, so the best buy order is the one with the highest bid price.

flashcards.front: For order matching, the best sell order is the one with the:
flashcards.back:
Lowest price

The system wants to match buyers with the lowest possible prices offered by sellers, so the best sell order is the one with the lowest ask price.

flashcards.front: What is the lowest level of user hierarchy in a trading firm's futures and options trading software, with access only to their own orders and trades?
flashcards.back:
Dealer

The dealer is at the lowest level in the internal hierarchy, typically able to manage trades only for the clients or accounts assigned to them.

flashcards.front: What is an order which is valid only for the single trading day on which it is entered?
flashcards.back:
Day order

A Day order is automatically cancelled by the trading system if it is not executed by the end of the trading session.

flashcards.front: What type of order allows a user to buy or sell a contract immediately, with any unmatched portion automatically cancelled?
flashcards.back:
IOC order

An Immediate or Cancel (IOC) order requires execution immediately upon entering the system, either fully or partially, with the rest cancelled.

flashcards.front: What is an order to buy or sell a contract at a specified price or a better price?
flashcards.back:
Limit order

A Limit order will only execute at the specified price or at a more favourable price for the trader.

flashcards.front: What is an order to buy or sell a contract at the best bid/offer price currently available in the market?
flashcards.back:
Market order

A Market order prioritizes execution speed, aiming to fill the order immediately at whatever price is currently available in the order book.

flashcards.front: What type of order gets activated when a trigger price is reached or crossed, and then enters the market as a market or limit order?
flashcards.back:
Stop-loss order

A Stop-loss order is designed to limit potential losses by automatically placing a trade once a specific price level is breached.

flashcards.front: If the put open interest exceeds the call open interest (PCR > 1), it is typically taken as a:
flashcards.back:
Bullish signal

A high PCR can suggest that more participants are betting on a price decline (buying puts) or selling puts, which could be interpreted as a bullish signal (selling puts implies expecting prices not to fall significantly).

flashcards.front: If the call open interest exceeds the put open interest (PCR < 1), it is typically taken as a:
flashcards.back:
Bearish signal

A low PCR can suggest that more participants are betting on a price increase (buying calls) or selling calls, which could be interpreted as a bearish signal (selling calls implies expecting prices not to rise significantly).

flashcards.front: What type of option spread involves options of the same type, having the same strike price, but different expiry dates?
flashcards.back:
Horizontal Spread or Calendar Spread

A horizontal or calendar spread compares options on the same underlying with the same strike price but different expiration dates.

flashcards.front: Which option spread involves a combination of options on the same underlying but different expiry dates as well as different strikes?
flashcards.back:
Diagonal Spread

A diagonal spread is a combination of vertical and horizontal spreads, featuring different strike prices and different expiration dates.

flashcards.front: What strategy involves buying a call and a put option with the same strike prices and same maturity?
flashcards.back:
Long Straddle

A long straddle is implemented by simultaneously purchasing a call and a put option on the same asset with identical strike prices and expiration dates.

flashcards.front: A long straddle strategy is undertaken when the trader expects:
flashcards.back:
A significant movement in the price of the underlying in either direction

This strategy profits from volatility, as it requires a large price move (either up or down) for the combined gain to exceed the cost of the premiums paid.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum loss for a long straddle position?
flashcards.back:
The sum of the premiums paid

The worst-case scenario for a long straddle is when the underlying price finishes exactly at the strike price at expiry, resulting in both options expiring worthless.

flashcards.front: A long straddle position typically has how many break-even points (BEPs)?
flashcards.back:
Two

A long straddle has an upper break-even point (Strike + Total Premium) and a lower break-even point (Strike - Total Premium).

flashcards.front: Which option among ITM, ATM, and OTM typically has the highest intrinsic value?
flashcards.back:
Deep ITM

Deep in-the-money options have the largest difference between the spot price and the strike price in the money-making direction.

flashcards.front: Which option among ITM, ATM, and OTM typically has only time value and no intrinsic value?
flashcards.back:
ATM and OTM

Only In-the-money options have intrinsic value; At-the-money and Out-of-the-money options derive their value solely from time and expected volatility (time value).

flashcards.front: In India, all index and stock options are typically which style of options?
flashcards.back:
European style

The text explicitly states that index and stock options traded on exchanges in India are European style, meaning exercise is only allowed on expiry.

flashcards.front: The party taking a long position, i.e., buying the option, is called the:
flashcards.back:
Holder

The buyer of an option is also referred to as the holder of the option, possessing the right to exercise the contract.

flashcards.front: The party taking a short position, i.e., selling the option, is called the:
flashcards.back:
Writer

The seller of an option is also known as the writer, receiving the premium and taking on the obligation if the buyer decides to exercise.

flashcards.front: If a person is short a futures contract at Rs. 100 and the underlying price at expiry is Rs. 60, what is the payoff per unit?
flashcards.back:
-Rs. 40

The seller is obligated to sell at 100. If the market price is 60, they can buy at 60 and sell at 100, gaining the difference (100 - 60).

flashcards.front: If a person is short a futures contract at Rs. 100 and the underlying price at expiry is Rs. 120, what is the payoff per unit?
flashcards.back:
-Rs. 20

The seller is obligated to sell at 100. If the market price is 120, they must buy at 120 to fulfill the contract, losing the difference (100 - 120).

flashcards.front: A contract whose value is derived from the value of another asset is known as a:
flashcards.back:
Derivative contract

A derivative's price is based on another asset, like shares, commodities, or indices, not a direct cash or spot trade.

flashcards.front: Which of the following is NOT typically an underlying asset for a derivative contract?
flashcards.back:
Financial statements

Derivatives are based on assets like shares, currency, or commodities. Financial statements are reports, not assets derivatives are based on.

flashcards.front: Derivatives can be based on underlying assets such as metals, energy resources, agri commodities, and:
flashcards.back:
Financial assets

Derivatives commonly use financial assets like shares, bonds, or foreign exchange as their underlying basis.

flashcards.front: According to the provided text, in which century did European trade fairs feature sellers promising future delivery?
flashcards.back:
12th Century

The text mentions European trade fairs in the 12th Century where contracts were signed for future delivery of goods sold.

flashcards.front: English Cistercian Monasteries in the 13th Century frequently sold their wool how far in advance using contracts?
flashcards.back:
Up to 20 years

In the 13th Century, English Cistercian Monasteries are noted for selling their wool up to 20 years ahead through contracts.

flashcards.front: The speculative boom and bust related to tulip futures in Holland occurred during which period?
flashcards.back:
1634-1637

The text specifically mentions the Tulip Mania in Holland happening between 1634 and 1637, involving tulip futures.

flashcards.front: In Japan, near Osaka, a futures market was developed at Dojima to protect producers from bad weather or warfare for which commodity?
flashcards.back:
Rice

A futures market in rice was developed at Dojima in Japan in the late 17th Century to protect rice producers.

flashcards.front: The Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) facilitated trading of forward contracts on various commodities starting in:
flashcards.back:
1848

The Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) began facilitating the trading of forward contracts on commodities in 1848.

flashcards.front: When did the CBOT list the first "exchange traded" derivative contract, known as a "futures contract," in the US?
flashcards.back:
1865

CBOT listed the first exchange traded derivative, called a futures contract, in the United States in 1865.

flashcards.front: The Chicago Butter and Egg Board, a spin-off of CBOT, was reorganised in 1919 to allow futures trading and later changed its name to:
flashcards.back:
Chicago Mercantile Exchange

The Chicago Butter and Egg Board was reorganised in 1919 and its name was later changed to the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME).

flashcards.front: In 1972, the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) introduced the International Monetary Market (IMM) for trading in:
flashcards.back:
Currency futures

The CME introduced the International Monetary Market (IMM) in 1972, which was created to allow trading in currency futures.

flashcards.front: Which was the first marketplace for trading listed options, introduced in 1973?
flashcards.back:
Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE)

The Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) became the first marketplace designed for trading listed options in 1973.

flashcards.front: The first successful pure interest rate futures contract, Treasury bill futures, was introduced in 1975 by:
flashcards.back:
CBOT

The CBOT introduced the Treasury bill futures contract in 1975, noted as the first successful pure interest rate futures.

flashcards.front: The Kansas City Board of Trade launched the first stock index futures in:
flashcards.back:
1982

In 1982, the Kansas City Board of Trade launched the first futures contract based on a stock index.

flashcards.front: Which indices were the basis for the first stock index options introduced by CBOE in 1983?
flashcards.back:
S&P 100 and S&P 500

CBOE introduced options on stock indices using the S&P 100 (OEX) and S&P 500 (SPXSM) Indices in 1983.

flashcards.front: Case: An index comprises three stocks: Stock A, Stock B, and Stock C. On January 1, 2024, their prices and shares outstanding were: Stock A: Rs. 100 (50 lakh shares), Stock B: Rs. 200 (30 lakh shares), Stock C: Rs. 150 (40 lakh shares). The base value of the index was set to 100 on January 1, 2024. On April 1, 2024, the prices changed to: Stock A: Rs. 120, Stock B: Rs. 210, Stock C: Rs. 160. The index is a market capitalization-weighted index. Calculate the market capitalization of the index on January 1, 2024.

Case: An index comprises three stocks: Stock A, Stock B, and Stock C. On January 1, 2024, their prices and shares outstanding were: Stock A: Rs. 100 (50 lakh shares), Stock B: Rs. 200 (30 lakh shares), Stock C: Rs. 150 (40 lakh shares). The base value of the index was set to 100 on January 1, 2024. On April 1, 2024, the prices changed to: Stock A: Rs. 120, Stock B: Rs. 210, Stock C: Rs. 160. The index is a market capitalization-weighted index. Calculate the new value of the index on April 1, 2024.

Case: A trader takes a long position in a Nifty futures contract on March 1, 2025, at Rs. 22,000. The lot size for Nifty futures is 25. The initial margin charged by the broker is 10% of the contract value. On March 2, 2025, the Nifty futures contract closes at Rs. 22,150. What is the total contract value of the trader's position?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 5,50,000

Contract Value = Futures Price * Lot Size = 22,000 * 25 = Rs. 5,50,000.

flashcards.front: Case: A trader takes a long position in a Nifty futures contract on March 1, 2025, at Rs. 22,000. The lot size for Nifty futures is 25. The initial margin charged by the broker is 10% of the contract value. On March 2, 2025, the Nifty futures contract closes at Rs. 22,150. What is the Mark-to-Market (MTM) gain for the trader on March 2, 2025?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 3,750

MTM Gain = (Closing Price - Trade Price) * Lot Size = (22,150 - 22,000) * 25 = 150 * 25 = Rs. 3,750.

flashcards.front: Case: The Nifty 50 index is currently trading at 20,500. A European call option on Nifty 50 with a strike price of 20,400 has a premium of Rs. 180. The lot size for Nifty options is 50. What is the intrinsic value of this Nifty call option?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 100

Intrinsic Value of Call Option = Max (0, Spot Price - Strike Price) = Max (0, 20,500 - 20,400) = Rs. 100.

flashcards.front: Case: The Nifty 50 index is currently trading at 20,500. A European call option on Nifty 50 with a strike price of 20,400 has a premium of Rs. 180. The lot size for Nifty options is 50. What is the time value of this Nifty call option?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 80

Time Value = Option Premium - Intrinsic Value = 180 - 100 = Rs. 80.

flashcards.front: Case: An investor plans to buy 1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. on July 15, 20XX, for which the current spot price is Rs. 500. To hedge against a potential price rise, the investor goes long one XYZ futures contract expiring on July 27, 20XX, at a price of Rs. 505. The lot size of the contract is 1,000 shares. On July 15, 20XX, the investor buys the shares in the spot market at Rs. 530, and the futures contract is trading at Rs. 532. What is the gain on the investor's futures position?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 27,000

Gain on Futures Position = (Futures Sale Price - Futures Purchase Price) * Lot Size = (532 - 505) * 1,000 = 27 * 1,000 = Rs. 27,000.

flashcards.front: Case: An investor plans to buy 1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. on July 15, 20XX, for which the current spot price is Rs. 500. To hedge against a potential price rise, the investor goes long one XYZ futures contract expiring on July 27, 20XX, at a price of Rs. 505. The lot size of the contract is 1,000 shares. On July 15, 20XX, the investor buys the shares in the spot market at Rs. 530, and the futures contract is trading at Rs. 532. What is the net effective purchase price per share for the investor?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 503

Cost of Buying in Cash Market = 1,000 * 530 = Rs. 530,000. Net Cost = Cash Cost - Gain on Futures = 530,000 - 27,000 = Rs. 503,000. Net Cost per Share = 503,000 / 1,000 = Rs. 503.

flashcards.front: Case: The spot price of Stock B is Rs. 100. A one-month futures contract on Stock B is trading at Rs. 90. The contract multiplier is 200 shares. The arbitrageur can invest proceeds from cash market sales at 9% p.a. for one month. What is the immediate arbitrage profit per share from executing a reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 10

Immediate arbitrage profit = Spot Price - Futures Price = 100 - 90 = Rs. 10 per share.

flashcards.front: Case: The spot price of Stock B is Rs. 100. A one-month futures contract on Stock B is trading at Rs. 90. The contract multiplier is 200 shares. The arbitrageur can invest proceeds from cash market sales at 9% p.a. for one month. If the arbitrageur carries the position for one month, what is the total gain from this arbitrage, including interest earned, assuming continuous compounding?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 2,150.56

Immediate gain = 200 * 10 = Rs. 2,000. Interest earned on Rs. 20,000 (100 * 200 shares) at 9% p.a. for one month (1/12 years) continuously compounded = 20,000 * (e^(0.09 * 1/12) - 1) = 20,000 * (e^0.0075 - 1) = 20,000 * (1.007528 - 1) = 20,000 * 0.007528 = Rs. 150.56. Total gain = 2,000 + 150.56 = Rs. 2,150.56.

flashcards.front: Case: An investor purchases a stock in the cash market at Rs. 1,600. To protect against a fall in price, the investor simultaneously buys a put option with a strike price of Rs. 1,600, paying a premium of Rs. 20. What is the maximum possible loss for the investor with this protective put strategy?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 20

The maximum loss for a protective put is the premium paid for the put option. In this case, Rs. 20.

flashcards.front: Case: An investor purchases a stock in the cash market at Rs. 1,600. To protect against a fall in price, the investor simultaneously buys a put option with a strike price of Rs. 1,600, paying a premium of Rs. 20. If the stock price rises to Rs. 1,660 at expiry, what is the net profit for the investor from this strategy?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 40

Profit on Long Cash = 1,660 - 1,600 = 60. Loss on Long Put = -20 (since it expires worthless). Net Profit = 60 - 20 = Rs. 40.

flashcards.front: Case: Clearing Member A has two Trading Members (TMs), PQR and XYZ. For the Nifty May contract, PQR has a proprietary net long position of 2,000 contracts, Client 1 (PQR) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (PQR) has a net long position of 2,000 contracts. TM XYZ has a proprietary net short position of 1,000 contracts, Client 1 (XYZ) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (XYZ) has a net short position of 1,000 contracts. What is the total long open position for TM PQR?
flashcards.back:
5,000 contracts

PQR's Long Position = Proprietary Net Long (2,000) + Client 1 Net Long (1,000) + Client 2 Net Long (2,000) = 5,000 contracts.

flashcards.front: Case: Clearing Member A has two Trading Members (TMs), PQR and XYZ. For the Nifty May contract, PQR has a proprietary net long position of 2,000 contracts, Client 1 (PQR) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (PQR) has a net long position of 2,000 contracts. TM XYZ has a proprietary net short position of 1,000 contracts, Client 1 (XYZ) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (XYZ) has a net short position of 1,000 contracts. What is the total short open position for Clearing Member A?
flashcards.back:
2,000 contracts

TM XYZ's Short Position = Proprietary Net Short (1,000) + Client 2 Net Short (1,000) = 2,000 contracts. TM PQR has no short position. Total Short Position for Clearing Member A = 0 + 2,000 = 2,000 contracts.

flashcards.front: Case: An investor sells an option in securities. The option premium is Rs. 50. Later, the option is exercised, and the settlement price is Rs. 1,500. What is the Securities Transaction Tax (STT) payable on the sale of this option?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 0.05

STT on sale of an option = 0.10% of option premium = 0.0010 * 50 = Rs. 0.05.

flashcards.front: Case: An investor sells an option in securities. The option premium is Rs. 50. Later, the option is exercised, and the settlement price is Rs. 1,500. What is the Securities Transaction Tax (STT) payable if this option is exercised?
flashcards.back:
Rs. 1.875

STT on sale of an option where option is exercised = 0.125% of the settlement price = 0.00125 * 1,500 = Rs. 1.875.

flashcards.front: Case: A portfolio consists of three stocks: Stock X, Stock Y, and Stock Z, with respective betas of 0.8, 1.2, and 1.5. The investment proportions (weights) in the portfolio are 40% for Stock X, 30% for Stock Y, and 30% for Stock Z. The market value of the portfolio is Rs. 1,00,00,000. The index futures price is 20,000, and the lot size is 50. What is the beta of this portfolio?

Case: A portfolio consists of three stocks: Stock X, Stock Y, and Stock Z, with respective betas of 0.8, 1.2, and 1.5. The investment proportions (weights) in the portfolio are 40% for Stock X, 30% for Stock Y, and 30% for Stock Z. The market value of the portfolio is Rs. 1,00,00,000. The index futures price is 20,000, and the lot size is 50. How many index futures contracts are required to hedge this portfolio's systematic risk (rounded to the nearest whole number)?
flashcards.back:
11 contracts

Number of contracts = (Value of Portfolio * Portfolio Beta) / (Index Futures Level * Contract Multiplier) = (1,00,00,000 * 1.07) / (20,000 * 50) = 1,07,00,000 / 10,00,000 = 10.7 contracts. Rounded to the nearest whole number, this is 11 contracts.

flashcards.front: Which is an example of a calendar spread?
flashcards.back:
Buy one-month futures and sell another month futures

Calendar spread involves buying and selling futures contracts of different expiry months.

flashcards.front: An index option is a __________________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Derivative product

An index option derives its value from an underlying index like Nifty or Sensex. It is classified as a derivative product, not a debt or cash market instrument.

flashcards.front: The purchase of a share in one market and the simultaneous sale in a different market to benefit from price differentials is known as ____________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Arbitrage

Arbitrage means buying in one market and simultaneously selling in another to profit from price differences. It is risk-free and exploits market inefficiencies.

flashcards.front: Financial derivatives provide the facility for __________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
All of the above

Derivatives serve multiple purposes — they allow trading (speculation), hedging (risk management), and arbitraging (exploiting price differences across markets).

flashcards.front: Operational risks include losses due to ____________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Inadequate disaster planning

Operational risk arises from inadequate internal processes, systems, or external events — such as poor disaster planning. Tax/government factors are regulatory risks.

flashcards.front: State whether TRUE or FALSE: Impact cost is low when the liquidity in the system is poor.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
False

Impact cost is inversely related to liquidity. When liquidity is poor, buying/selling large quantities moves prices more, making impact cost HIGH, not low.

flashcards.front: Which of the following costs is not actually paid by the market participants but arises due to lack of liquidity? (NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Impact cost

Impact cost is an implicit cost — it is not paid directly but reflects the price movement caused by large orders in an illiquid market. STT and brokerage are explicit costs.

flashcards.front: You sold one XYZ Stock Futures contract at Rs. 278 and the lot size is 1,200. What is your profit (+) or loss (-), if you purchase the contract back at Rs. 265?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
15,600

Profit = (Sell price - Buy price) x Lot size = (278 - 265) x 1200 = 13 x 1200 = Rs. 15,600 profit (short position closed at lower price).

flashcards.front: You have taken a short position of one contract in June XYZ futures (contract multiplier 50) at a price of Rs. 3,400. When you closed this position, you made a profit of Rs. 10,000. Which closing action enabled this profit?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Buying 1 June XYZ futures contract at 3200

Short position profits when price falls. Profit = (3400 - 3200) x 50 = 200 x 50 = Rs. 10,000. Buying back at 3200 closes the short with correct profit.

flashcards.front: A calendar spread contract in index futures attracts ___________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Lower margin than sum of two independent legs of futures contract

Calendar spreads have offsetting positions, so risk is lower. Hence, exchanges charge lower margin compared to two independent (unhedged) futures positions.

flashcards.front: Margins in 'Futures' trading are to be paid by _______.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Both the buyer and the seller

In futures trading, both buyers and sellers are obligated to pay margins. This ensures both parties honor their contractual commitments.

flashcards.front: When the near leg of the calendar spread transaction on index futures expires, the farther leg becomes a regular open position. (NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
True

Once the near-month contract expires, the hedge is gone. The far-month contract then stands alone as an independent (regular open) position, attracting full margin.

flashcards.front: The buyer of an option cannot lose more than the option premium paid. (NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
True for all options

Option buyers pay a premium upfront. Their maximum loss is always limited to this premium paid, regardless of whether the option is European or American.

flashcards.front: You sold a Put option on a share. The strike price of the put was Rs. 245 and you received a premium of Rs. 49. Theoretically, what can be the maximum loss on this position?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
196

Put seller's max loss occurs if stock falls to zero. Loss = Strike price - Premium received = 245 - 49 = Rs. 196. Premium received partially offsets downside.

flashcards.front: Current Price of XYZ Stock is Rs 286. Rs. 260 strike call is quoted at Rs 45. What is the Intrinsic Value?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
26

Intrinsic value of a call = Current Price - Strike Price = 286 - 260 = Rs. 26. The Rs. 45 premium includes intrinsic value (26) plus time value (19).

flashcards.front: A European call option gives the buyer the right but not the obligation to buy from the seller an underlying at the prevailing market price "on or before" the expiry date.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
False

A European option can only be exercised ON the expiry date, not before. "On or before" describes an American option. The exercise price is also the strike price, not market price.

flashcards.front: An in-the-money option is _____________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
An option with a positive intrinsic value

An in-the-money option has positive intrinsic value — a call is ITM when market price > strike; a put is ITM when market price < strike.

flashcards.front: If an investor buys a call option with lower strike price and sells another call option with higher strike price, both on the same underlying share and same expiration date, the strategy is called ___________(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Bullish spread

Buying a lower-strike call and selling a higher-strike call on the same expiry creates a Bull Call Spread (Bullish spread), profiting when the underlying price rises.

flashcards.front: Which of the following is a hedged position?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Protective put

A protective put involves holding a stock and buying a put option, which hedges downside risk. Covered call provides partial hedge; short straddle/strangle are unhedged strategies.

flashcards.front: Put-call parity refers to the relationship between: ________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Call and put options on the same stock with the same strike prices and same maturity

Put-call parity defines the price relationship between a call and put option on the same underlying, with identical strike price and expiration date.

flashcards.front: Which of the following situations indicates a bullish trend in the underlying?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
A rising futures price along with rising open interest

Rising futures price + rising open interest = new money entering long positions = bullish signal. It confirms upward momentum backed by fresh buying interest.

flashcards.front: On the derivative exchanges, all the orders entered on the Trading System are at prices exclusive of brokerage.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
True

On derivative exchanges, order prices entered on the trading system exclude brokerage. Brokerage is charged separately by the broker outside the exchange system.

flashcards.front: A trader has bought 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 780 per share. He expects the price to go up but wants to protect himself if the price falls. He does not want to lose more than Rs. 1000 on this long position in XYZ. What should the trader do?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Place a stop loss sell order for 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 770 per share

A stop-loss sell order at Rs 770 triggers if price falls to 770, limiting loss to (780-770) x 100 = Rs 1,000. This protects against downside while holding the long position.

flashcards.front: Trader A wants to sell 20 contracts of August series at Rs 4500 and Trader B wants to sell 17 contracts of September series at Rs 4550. Lot size is 50. The Initial Margin is fixed at 6%. How much Initial Margin is required from both investors combined? (NISM workbook)

A member has two clients C1 and C2. C1 has purchased 800 contracts and C2 has sold 900 contracts in August XYZ futures series. What is the outstanding liability (open position) of the member towards Clearing Corporation?(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
1700

Client positions are NOT netted against each other. Open position = C1's long (800) + C2's short (900) = 1700 contracts is the gross open position reported to Clearing Corporation.

flashcards.front: Clients' positions cannot be netted off against each other while calculating initial margin on the derivatives segment. (NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
True

Each client's position is treated independently for margin purposes. Two clients' opposite positions cannot be offset — each client must maintain their own margin separately.

flashcards.front: Mark-to-market margins are collected ___________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
On a daily basis

MTM margins are settled daily. At end of each trading day, gains/losses on open futures positions are calculated and paid/received by members the next morning.

flashcards.front: On the Governing Council of the Clearing Corporation of the derivatives segment, broker-members are allowed.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
False

To avoid conflict of interest, broker-members are NOT permitted on the Governing Council of the Clearing Corporation. Independent/public interest directors govern it.

flashcards.front: The main objective of Trade Guarantee Fund (TGF) at the exchanges is _________________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
All of the above

TGF serves all three purposes — guaranteeing settlements, building market confidence, and protecting investors. It is the exchange's safety net for trade settlements.

flashcards.front: A penalty or suspension of registration of a stock broker from derivatives exchange/segment under the SEBI (Stock Broker) Regulations, 1992 can take place if _______________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
In any of the above situations

SEBI can penalize or suspend a broker for any of these violations — non-payment of fees, breach of registration conditions, or exchange suspension.

flashcards.front: A defaulting member's clients' positions could be transferred to ____________ by the Clearing Corporation.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Another solvent member

When a member defaults, the Clearing Corporation transfers client positions to another solvent (financially sound) member to protect clients' interests and ensure continuity.

flashcards.front: Profits from derivatives transactions for Indian investors are taxed as: __________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Speculative income under the head 'profits and gains of business or profession'

Under Indian tax law, F&O income is classified as non-speculative business income under 'profits and gains of business or profession', allowing set-off against business losses.

flashcards.front: In the case of Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs), the gains or losses arising from derivatives transactions on a recognized stock exchange are taxable as:(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Short term capital gain

For FPIs, derivatives gains/losses on recognized exchanges are treated as short-term capital gains, unlike domestic investors for whom it is business income.

flashcards.front: When establishing a relationship with a new client, the trading member takes reasonable steps to assess the background, genuineness, beneficial identify, financial soundness of such person and his investment/trading objectives.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
True

This is part of the KYC (Know Your Customer) process. Trading members are required to verify client identity, financial background, and objectives before onboarding.

flashcards.front: In the KYC process, Politically Exposed Persons are termed as:(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Clients of Special Categories

Under KYC/AML regulations, Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) — those holding prominent public positions — fall under 'Clients of Special Categories' requiring enhanced due diligence.

flashcards.front: _________ is allotted on client onboarding and serves as an exclusive identification of the client.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Unique client code

A Unique Client Code (UCC) is assigned to every client at onboarding. It exclusively identifies the client across all transactions on the exchange.

flashcards.front: Under the Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Combating of Financial Terrorism (CFT) regulations, suspicious transactions must be reported to ___________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
Financial Intelligence Unit - India

Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) must be filed with the Financial Intelligence Unit - India (FIU-IND), the national agency that collects and analyses financial intelligence.

flashcards.front: SCORES is: __________.(NISM workbook)
flashcards.back:
SEBI's web-based complaints redressal system

SCORES (SEBI Complaints Redress System) is SEBI's online platform for investors to lodge complaints against listed companies or registered intermediaries and track resolution.

flashcards.front: Who benefits when the impact cost in a stock transaction is very high?
flashcards.back:
Neither buyers nor sellers

Impact cost is the extra cost due to low liquidity. A high impact cost increases the buying price and reduces the selling price. Therefore, both buyers and sellers are negatively affected.

flashcards.front: Initial margin in derivatives is generally higher when ______.
flashcards.back:
Volatility is high

When volatility is high, prices fluctuate more. This increases risk. Therefore, exchanges collect higher initial margins to protect against possible losses.

flashcards.front: Which Greek measures the change in option premium for a one unit change in volatility?
flashcards.back:
Vega

Vega shows how much the option price changes when volatility changes. Higher volatility usually increases option premium.

flashcards.front: In a derivatives exchange, clearing members must maintain higher net worth than non-clearing members.
flashcards.back:
True

Clearing members handle settlement risk and guarantee trades. Hence, they are required to maintain higher net worth.

flashcards.front: Which risk cannot be eliminated by diversification?
flashcards.back:
Unsystematic Risk

Systematic risk affects the entire market (like inflation, policy changes). It cannot be removed by diversification.

flashcards.front: Can a long position in a Put option be squared off by taking a short position in a Call option with the same strike price and expiry?
flashcards.back:
No

A long Put can only be closed by selling the same Put contract. A Call option is a different contract and cannot offset the Put.

flashcards.front: If the price of the underlying asset rises after entering a futures contract, what happens to the long position?
flashcards.back:
It becomes profitable

A long futures position benefits when price rises. Profit = Selling Price – Buying Price.

flashcards.front: Is it possible to earn high returns without taking any risk?
flashcards.back:
False

Higher returns always come with higher risk. No investment gives guaranteed high returns without risk.

flashcards.front: What is the approximate Delta of a far out-of-the-money option?
flashcards.back:
Near 0

Out-of-the-money options have very little chance of expiring in profit. Hence, their Delta is close to zero.

flashcards.front: What does "Mark to Market" mean in derivatives trading?
flashcards.back:
Daily revaluation of open positions

Mark to Market (MTM) means daily calculation of profit or loss based on market price changes. Losses are collected and profits are credited daily.

flashcards.front: The margining system for index futures is based on ______.
flashcards.back:
Value at Risk

As per regulatory guidelines, margins for index futures are calculated using the Value at Risk (VaR) method. VaR estimates the maximum expected loss at a given confidence level.

flashcards.front: Mr. Ashu bought 100 shares at Rs 980 each. He does not want to lose more than Rs 1,000. What should he do?
flashcards.back:
Place stop loss at Rs 970

Maximum Loss Allowed = Rs 1,000 Shares = 100 Loss per Share = 1000 ÷ 100 = Rs 10 Stop Loss Price = 980 – 10 = Rs 970 If price falls to Rs 970, loss per share is Rs 10. Total loss = 10 × 100 = Rs 1,000.

flashcards.front: A call option gives the buyer the right to buy the underlying asset at market price.
flashcards.back:
False

A call option gives the right to buy at the strike price, not the market price. The strike price is fixed in the contract.

flashcards.front: A trader buys a futures contract at Rs 768. Lot size is 1200. He squares off at Rs 778. What is his profit or loss?
flashcards.back:
Rs 12,000 Profit

Profit per unit = 778 – 768 = Rs 10 Total Profit = 10 × 1200 = Rs 12,000. Since price increased, the buyer makes profit of Rs 12,000.

flashcards.front: A Professional Clearing Member in derivatives segment ______.
flashcards.back:
Has no trading rights

A Professional Clearing Member only clears and settles trades. They do not have rights to trade on the exchange.

flashcards.front: Theta measures sensitivity of option price to change in volatility.
flashcards.back:
False

Theta measures time decay (change in price due to passage of time). Vega measures sensitivity to volatility.

flashcards.front: Which complaint can an Exchange resolve against a trading member?
flashcards.back:
Unauthorized trade in client account

Exchanges can resolve complaints related to trading issues like unauthorized transactions in a client’s account.

flashcards.front: Clearing and settlement of derivatives trades in India is done through ______.
flashcards.back:
SEBI Approved Clearing Corporation

All derivatives trades are cleared and settled through SEBI-approved Clearing Corporations.

flashcards.front: Delta measures change in option price due to change in interest rate.
flashcards.back:
False

Rho measures sensitivity to interest rate. Delta measures change in option price due to change in underlying asset price.

flashcards.front: Can a Clearing Member provide fixed deposits as part of liquid assets to Clearing Corporation?
flashcards.back:
Yes

Fixed deposits are accepted as liquid assets along with cash, government securities, and bank guarantees for margin requirements.

flashcards.front: Vega measures change in Delta due to change in price of underlying asset.
flashcards.back:
False

Vega measures sensitivity of option price to volatility. Gamma measures change in Delta with respect to change in underlying price.

flashcards.front: A short position in a Put option can be closed by taking a long position in the same Put option (same strike and expiry).
flashcards.back:
True

A short Put can be squared off by buying the same Put contract with identical strike price and expiry date.

flashcards.front: On final settlement of an option, the favorable difference received by the buyer is recorded as ______ in Profit & Loss Account.
flashcards.back:
Income

If the option is exercised profitably, the difference received is treated as income in the Profit & Loss account.

flashcards.front: Higher the strike price, lower will be the premium of a Call option.
flashcards.back:
True

As strike price increases, the intrinsic value of the call decreases. Therefore, the premium generally becomes lower.

flashcards.front: Trading in Indian equity derivatives is allowed in ______.
flashcards.back:
All of the above

Indian equity derivatives market allows trading in index options, stock options, and stock futures.

flashcards.front: Can the exercise price of an option be above, below, or equal to the current spot price?
flashcards.back:
Yes

Strike price is fixed in the contract, while spot price changes. So, strike price can be higher, lower, or equal to spot price.

flashcards.front: Can a person sell futures contracts without owning the underlying asset?
flashcards.back:
Yes

Futures trading does not require ownership of the underlying asset. Only margin payment is required.

flashcards.front: If three futures contracts of 1 month, 2 months, and 3 months are available, how many calendar spreads can be created?
flashcards.back:
3

Possible combinations: 1 & 2, 2 & 3, 1 & 3 Total = 3 spreads Calendar spread involves contracts of different expiry months. Three combinations are possible.

flashcards.front: All 50 stocks in NIFTY 50 index are equally weighted.
flashcards.back:
False

NIFTY 50 uses free-float market capitalization method. Stocks are weighted based on market cap, not equally.

flashcards.front: A Call option gives the buyer ______.
flashcards.back:
Right only

A Call option gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy the underlying asset at the strike price.

flashcards.front: Speculators take risk while hedgers try to reduce risk.
flashcards.back:
True

Hedgers use derivatives to reduce or transfer risk. Speculators accept risk in expectation of profit from price movements.

flashcards.front: Money and securities deposited by clients can be used to meet broker’s proprietary obligations.
flashcards.back:
Cannot be attached

Client funds and securities must be kept separate. They cannot be used to meet the broker’s personal or proprietary obligations.

flashcards.front: If a stock that is part of an index undergoes a stock split, what happens to the index value?
flashcards.back:
It remains unchanged

Stock split increases number of shares but reduces price proportionally. Total market capitalization remains same, so index value is unaffected.

flashcards.front: Time decay is beneficial to the ______.
flashcards.back:
Option Seller

As time passes, option premium reduces due to time decay. This benefits the option seller because he has already received the premium.

flashcards.front: Identify the correct statement about a Put option.
flashcards.back:
Buyer has right but not obligation to sell

A Put option gives the buyer the right (not obligation) to sell the underlying asset at a fixed strike price.

flashcards.front: Mr. Menon bought a futures contract and price rises. What will happen?
flashcards.back:
He makes profit

In futures, profit = Selling Price – Buying Price. If price rises after buying, the buyer gains.

flashcards.front: When a forward contract is used for hedging, the premium or discount is ______.
flashcards.back:
Amortized over life of contract

As per accounting standards, forward premium or discount for hedging is spread (amortized) over the contract period.

flashcards.front: Derivatives market transfers risk from ______.
flashcards.back:
Hedgers to Speculators

Hedgers want to reduce risk. Speculators are willing to take risk for profit. So risk moves from hedgers to speculators.

flashcards.front: Which is an example of a calendar spread?
flashcards.back:
Buy one-month futures and sell another month futures

Calendar spread involves buying and selling futures contracts of different expiry months.

flashcards.front: To sell in futures market, a person ______.
flashcards.back:
Need not own underlying

In futures market, ownership of underlying asset is not required. Only margin payment is necessary.

flashcards.front: The derivatives market helps in ______.
flashcards.back:
Both A and B

Derivatives shift risk from hedgers (low risk appetite) to speculators (high risk appetite) and help in better risk distribution in the market.

flashcards.front: Identify the FALSE statement about options.
flashcards.back:
Options have linear payoff

Options have non-linear payoff. Profit or loss does not move in straight line like futures. Buyer has limited loss and unlimited profit (in call).

flashcards.front: ‘SCORES’ is the name of ______.
flashcards.back:
SEBI’s Online Complaint Redressal System

SCORES (SEBI Complaints Redress System) is SEBI’s web-based system for investor grievance redressal.

flashcards.front: If a trading member reaches prescribed position limits, he will ______.
flashcards.back:
Open new positions

When position limits are reached, fresh positions cannot be opened. However, existing positions can be reduced or reversed.

flashcards.front: If the near-month contract in a calendar spread expires, the far-month contract becomes a ______.
flashcards.back:
Naked position

When one leg of spread expires, only one contract remains. This becomes a naked (open) position.

flashcards.front: A clearing member can close out transactions of a trading member if ______.
flashcards.back:
Both A and B

Failure to pay margin or settlement dues is a serious violation. Clearing Corporation can close positions to control risk.

flashcards.front: Which contract is settled bilaterally (not through exchange)?
flashcards.back:
Three-month Forward Contract

Forward contracts are private agreements between two parties and are settled bilaterally, not through an exchange.

flashcards.front: If volatility of underlying decreases, the premium of a Call option will ______.
flashcards.back:
Decrease

Higher volatility increases option premium. Lower volatility reduces option premium.

flashcards.front: A European Call option can be exercised ______.
flashcards.back:
Only on expiry date

European options can be exercised only on the expiry date. In India, stock and index options follow European style.

flashcards.front: In futures trading, margin is paid by ______.
flashcards.back:
Both buyer and seller

In futures contracts, both buyer and seller must deposit initial margin. In options, only seller pays margin while buyer pays premium.

flashcards.front: A financial agreement that tracks its price based on the value of a different asset is known as a:
flashcards.back:
Derivative contract

Derivatives do not have their own value; they get their price from another asset like shares or gold.

flashcards.front: Which of the following is NOT a valid base for a derivative contract?
flashcards.back:
A company's secret strategy meeting

Derivatives must be based on tradeable assets or measurable financial variables, not private information.

flashcards.front: Historically, during which century were future-delivery deals first seen at European trade fairs?
flashcards.back:
12th Century

The sources mention that contracts for goods to be delivered later were signed at fairs in the 12th Century.

flashcards.front: In the 13th Century, how far into the future did English Monasteries sell their wool using contracts?
flashcards.back:
20 years

Monasteries in England used to pre-sell their wool as far as 20 years in advance.

flashcards.front: What was the famous financial "bubble" involving flower futures in Holland during the 1630s?
flashcards.back:
Tulip Mania

Between 1634 and 1637, Holland experienced a speculative boom and bust involving tulip futures.

flashcards.front: Where was the first organized rice futures market created to protect farmers from weather risks?
flashcards.back:
Dojima, Japan

Japan developed a rice futures market in the late 17th Century to help farmers manage risks from weather or war.

flashcards.front: Which exchange was founded in 1848 to bring standard rules to grain trading?
flashcards.back:
Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT)

The CBOT was created in 1848 to standardize forward contracts for commodities like grain.

flashcards.front: In what year did the first "futures contract" get officially listed on an exchange in the USA?
flashcards.back:
1865

While CBOT started in 1848, the first standardized "futures" contract was listed in 1865.

flashcards.front: The Chicago Butter and Egg Board eventually became known by what modern name?
flashcards.back:
Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME)

This board was reorganized in 1919 and eventually became the CME.

flashcards.front: What type of futures did the CME launch in 1972 under the International Monetary Market?
flashcards.back:
Currency futures

The IMM was established in 1972 specifically for trading currency futures.

flashcards.front: Which organization launched the first marketplace specifically for trading listed options in 1973?
flashcards.back:
CBOE

The Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) was the first to offer listed options trading.

flashcards.front: An investor holds an equity portfolio valued at Rs. 7,000,000 with a beta of 0.9. The Nifty Index is currently trading at 18200. If the lot size of Nifty futures is 50, how many contracts of Nifty futures must the investor short to hedge the portfolio against systematic risk?
flashcards.back:
7 contracts

Number of contracts = (Portfolio Value * Beta) / (Nifty Futures Price * Lot Size) = (7000000 * 0.9) / (18200 * 50) = 7 contracts.

flashcards.front: Calculate the fair value of a stock futures contract if the spot price of the stock is Rs. 550, the risk-free interest rate is 6.5% per annum, the time to expiration is 35 days, and the expected dividend to be paid before expiry is Rs. 2.50 per share.
flashcards.back:
Rs. 550.93

Fair Value = Spot Price + Interest Cost - Dividend. Interest Cost = Rs. 550 * 0.0650 * 35/365 = Rs. 3.43. Fair Price = 550 + 3.43 - 2.50 = Rs. 550.93.

flashcards.front: Calculate the fair value of a stock futures contract if the spot price of the stock is Rs. 600, the risk-free interest rate is 7.0% per annum, the time to expiration is 40 days, and the expected dividend to be paid before expiry is Rs. 3.00 per share.

flashcards.front: A trader buys 1 contract of Nifty futures (lot size 50) at Rs. 15,100. The closing price on Day 1 is Rs. 14,960 and the closing price on Day 2 is Rs. 15,195. What is the Mark-to-Market (MTM) margin impact on Day 1 and Day 2?
flashcards.back:
Day 1: Rs. -7,000 | Day 2: Rs. +11,750

MTM Day 1 = (Day 1 Close - Buy Price) * Lot Size = (14960 - 15100) * 50 = Rs. -7,000. MTM Day 2 = (Day 2 Close - Day 1 Close) * Lot Size = (15195 - 14960) * 50 = Rs. 11,750.

flashcards.front: A trader buys 1 contract of Nifty futures (lot size 50) at Rs. 15,200. The closing price on Day 1 is Rs. 15,070 and the closing price on Day 2 is Rs. 15,290. What is the Mark-to-Market (MTM) margin impact on Day 1 and Day 2?

flashcards.front: An investor constructs a Bull Call Spread by buying a Call Option at strike Rs. 17200 for a premium of Rs. 210 and selling a Call Option at strike Rs. 17600 for a premium of Rs. 85. Calculate the maximum loss, maximum profit, and the break-even point for this strategy.
flashcards.back:
Max Loss: Rs. 125 | Max Profit: Rs. 275 | Break-even: Rs. 17325

Net Cost (Max Loss) = Premium Paid - Premium Received = 210 - 85 = Rs. 125. Max Profit = (Difference in Strike Prices) - Net Debit = (17600 - 17200) - 125 = Rs. 275. Break-even = Lower Strike + Net Debit = 17200 + 125 = Rs. 17325.

flashcards.front: Which of the following describes the 'Standardized Portfolio Analysis of Risk' (SPAN) margin system?
flashcards.back:
It calculates margins based on the worst-case scenario loss of the portfolio across a set of scenarios

SPAN is a portfolio-based margin system that evaluates the risk of a portfolio as a whole by simulating 16 risk scenarios based on changes in underlying price and volatility.

flashcards.front: What happens to the Delta of an Out-of-the-Money (OTM) Call option as the expiry approaches, assuming other factors remain constant?
flashcards.back:
It approaches 0

As expiry approaches, the probability of an OTM option becoming in-the-money decreases. Therefore, its sensitivity to underlying price movement (Delta) drops towards 0.

flashcards.front: Which Option Greek is used to measure the sensitivity of Delta to the change in the price of the underlying asset?
flashcards.back:
Gamma

Gamma is the rate of change of Delta per unit change in the underlying asset's price. It is the second-order derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying price.

flashcards.front: In a liquid derivatives market, which of the following is true regarding impact cost?
flashcards.back:
Lower impact cost indicates higher liquidity

Impact cost represents the slippage or execution penalty of an order. A lower impact cost means the order can be executed with minimal price disruption, reflecting high liquidity.

flashcards.front: An option trader is net long on Vega. Which of the following market conditions will benefit this trader?
flashcards.back:
An increase in implied volatility

Vega measures the sensitivity of option premium to implied volatility. Option buyers (long Vega) benefit when implied volatility increases, as it inflates option values.

flashcards.front: Under the current SEBI guidelines, how is the Initial Margin for index options calculated?
flashcards.back:
SPAN margin based on 99% Value at Risk (VaR) over a 1-day horizon

SPAN margin is calculated based on VaR with a 99% confidence level over a 1-day time horizon to cover worst-case potential losses.

flashcards.front: What is the primary role of a 'Professional Clearing Member' (PCM) in the Indian derivatives segment?
flashcards.back:
To clear and settle trades of associate trading members and clients, without executing trades himself

A PCM is a clearing member who is not a trading member. They typically include banks or custodians who only clear and settle trades for other market participants.

flashcards.front: Under Section 43(5) of the Income Tax Act, transactions in equity derivatives on recognized stock exchanges are classified as:
flashcards.back:
Non-speculative business income

Transactions in equity derivatives executed on recognized stock exchanges are treated as non-speculative business transactions under Section 43(5) of the Income Tax Act.

flashcards.front: If a stock splits 1:2 (every 1 share becomes 2 shares), how does the exchange adjust the option contract strike price and lot size?
flashcards.back:
Strike price is halved, and lot size is doubled

To maintain the same contract value and moneyness, the strike price is divided by the split factor (halved), and the lot size is multiplied by the split factor (doubled).

flashcards.front: Which of the following committees was formed by SEBI in 1996 to develop the regulatory framework for derivatives in India?
flashcards.back:
Dr. L.C. Gupta Committee

SEBI set up the Dr. L.C. Gupta Committee in 1996 to formulate a regulatory framework for index futures and derivatives trading in India.

flashcards.front: Advanced Concept Question 51: Which of the following is true for a calendar spread position in futures contracts?
flashcards.back:
It involves buying a near-month contract and selling a far-month contract of the same underlying

A calendar spread involves taking simultaneous opposite positions in contracts of different expiries on the same underlying, reducing price risk and requiring lower margin.

flashcards.front: Advanced Concept Question 58: Which of the following is true for a calendar spread position in futures contracts?
flashcards.back:
It involves buying a near-month contract and selling a far-month contract of the same underlying

A calendar spread involves taking simultaneous opposite positions in contracts of different expiries on the same underlying, reducing price risk and requiring lower margin.

flashcards.front: An investor purchases a Put Option with a strike price of Rs. 1100 for a premium of Rs. 42. At expiry, the underlying spot price is Rs. 980. Calculate the intrinsic value of the option and the net profit or loss per unit for the buyer.
flashcards.back:
Intrinsic Value: Rs. 120 | Profit: Rs. 78

Intrinsic Value of Put = Max(0, Strike - Spot) = 1100 - 980 = Rs. 120. Net Profit/Loss = Intrinsic Value - Premium Paid = 120 - 42 = Rs. 78 per unit.

flashcards.front: A stock's spot price is Rs. 820. The 30-day stock futures contract trades at Rs. 822. If the risk-free rate is 6%, calculate the theoretical fair price. Is there an arbitrage opportunity, and what is the potential profit per unit if you do a cash-and-carry arbitrage?
flashcards.back:
Fair Price: Rs. 824.04 | Arbitrage Profit: Rs. -2.04 (Buy Spot, Sell Futures)

Theoretical Fair Futures Price = Spot * (1 + r * t) = 820 * (1 + 0.06 * 30/365) = Rs. 824.04. Since market futures price (822) > fair price (824.04), you can buy spot and short futures to lock in a risk-free profit of Rs. -2.04.

flashcards.front: A portfolio manager holds 10,000 shares of ABC Ltd. He wants to construct a Delta Neutral portfolio using Call options on ABC Ltd. which have a Delta of 0.42. How many Call options must he write (sell) to make the portfolio delta neutral?
flashcards.back:
Sell 23,810 Call options

To achieve delta neutrality, Portfolio Delta + Options Delta = 0. Shares have delta = +1. Delta of portfolio = 10000 * 1 = 10000. Written options have negative delta. Number of options to sell = Portfolio Delta / Option Delta = 10000 / 0.42 = 23,810 calls.
